primary.title: Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure)
primary.authors: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L

primary.title: Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction
primary.authors: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D

guidelines.title: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure

questions.stem: A patient has EF 30%, dilated LV, and S3 gallop. Classification?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF): LV EF ≤40%
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: statpearls-hf-ef: 'In heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, the left ventricle enlarges and weakens'
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: EF 30% is ≤40% → HFrEF. Dilated LV and S3 are consistent with systolic dysfunction (volume overload). S3 is caused by rapid ventricular filling in a dilated compliant ventricle.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: HFrEF ≤40% threshold is per 2022 guidelines. The current classification also includes HFmrEF (41-49%) and HFpEF (≥50%).

primary.supportingQuote: HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF): LV EF ≤40%

supporting.keyFinding: In heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, the left ventricle enlarges and weakens

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: None. All sources agree HFrEF = LVEF ≤40%.

questions.verifiedExplanation: An EF of 30% meets the definition of HFrEF (LVEF ≤40%) per the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guidelines. The dilated LV and S3 gallop are hallmarks of systolic dysfunction: the ventricle enlarges and weakens, leading to increased end-diastolic volume and rapid early filling that produces the S3 sound. This contrasts with HFpEF (LVEF ≥50%), where the ventricle is stiff rather than dilated and produces an S4 gallop from atrial contraction against a noncompliant ventricle. Restrictive cardiomyopathy shows normal or near-normal chamber size with severely impaired diastolic filling. Cor pulmonale is right-sided heart failure from pulmonary disease, not left ventricular systolic dysfunction. The current classification also recognizes HFmrEF (LVEF 41-49%) and HF with improved EF (previously ≤40%, now >40%).
verifiedReferences: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Which diuretic REDUCES MORTALITY in HFrEF?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRAs) are indicated for NYHA class II-IV patients with EF ≤35%, stable serum creatinine, eGFR >30 mL/min/1.73 m², and stable serum potassium <5.0 mEq/L.
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: statpearls-hf: 'For patients with congestion, diuretics improve symptoms and reduce HF progression' (re: loop diuretics - symptom relief only, NOT mortality). 'For patients with HFrEF, an ARNI is recommended to reduce mortality and morbidity.'
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: Key teaching point: spironolactone is classified as an MRA, not as a typical diuretic. Loop diuretics (furosemide) and thiazides (HCTZ, metolazone) improve symptoms but do NOT reduce mortality. Spironolactone/eplerenone are the only 'diuretics' with mortality benefit in HFrEF (RALES trial for spironolactone, EMPHASIS-HF for eplerenone).

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: MRAs remain part of the 'four pillars' of HFrEF GDMT. Spironolactone (RALES trial) and eplerenone (EMPHASIS-HF) both have mortality data.

primary.supportingQuote: Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRAs) are indicated for NYHA class II-IV patients with EF ≤35%

supporting.keyFinding: Loop diuretics relieve congestion and improve symptoms; Diuretics recommended in any heart failure with symptoms and signs suggestive of volume overload

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: None. All sources distinguish MRAs (mortality benefit) from loop/thiazide diuretics (symptom relief only).

questions.verifiedExplanation: Spironolactone (an MRA/mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) is the only option listed that reduces mortality in HFrEF. The RALES trial demonstrated a 30% mortality reduction with spironolactone in NYHA class III-IV HFrEF. MRAs are now one of the 'four pillars' of guideline-directed medical therapy for HFrEF (along with beta-blockers, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, and SGLT2 inhibitors). In contrast, loop diuretics (furosemide) relieve congestion and improve symptoms but have never shown mortality benefit — they are essential for volume management but are not disease-modifying. Metolazone and hydrochlorothiazide are thiazide diuretics used as adjuncts when patients are resistant to loop diuretics, also without mortality benefit. MRA prescribing requires monitoring: eGFR >30, potassium <5.0.
verifiedReferences: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]
issuesFound: Question stem says 'diuretic' which could be misleading — spironolactone is technically an MRA, not a traditional diuretic. However, this IS a board-style trap question testing exactly that distinction.

questions.stem: Only three beta-blockers are proven to reduce mortality in HFrEF. They are:

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Beta-blockers (Class I) reverse sympathetic activation in HFrEF, improving survival, reducing heart failure hospitalizations, and increasing left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF). Randomized controlled trials support Carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, and bisoprolol.
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: Exact match. StatPearls explicitly names these three. Critical distinction: metoprolol SUCCINATE (extended-release, MERIT-HF trial), NOT metoprolol tartrate.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: These three beta-blockers remain the only evidence-based choices per current guidelines.

primary.supportingQuote: Randomized controlled trials support Carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, and bisoprolol

questions.verifiedExplanation: Only three beta-blockers have RCT evidence for mortality reduction in HFrEF: carvedilol (COPERNICUS, CAPRICORN trials), metoprolol succinate (MERIT-HF trial), and bisoprolol (CIBIS-II trial). This is not a class effect — other beta-blockers have not shown mortality benefit and some may be harmful. Critical distinction: metoprolol succinate (extended-release) has the evidence, NOT metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release), which was used in the COMET trial and was inferior to carvedilol. Sotalol has antiarrhythmic properties but no HF mortality benefit. Atenolol, propranolol, and nadolol lack HFrEF outcome data. Beta-blockers should be started at low doses and titrated to target doses as tolerated, and should not be initiated during acute decompensation.
verifiedReferences: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: SGLT2 inhibitors in HFrEF benefit which patients?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors (SGLT2i) (Class 1A recommendation for HFrEF, Class 2A recommendation for HFpEF and HFmrEF) have shown positive results in reducing HF hospitalization and mortality.
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: The Class 1A recommendation for HFrEF is universal (not limited to diabetic patients). DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced trials both enrolled diabetic and non-diabetic patients and showed benefit in both groups.
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: StatPearls gives a Class 1A recommendation without restricting to diabetics. The contraindications listed are hypotension, SBP <95, eGFR <30 — not diabetes status.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: Class 1A for HFrEF regardless of diabetes status is current per 2022 guidelines.

primary.supportingQuote: SGLT2i (Class 1A recommendation for HFrEF) have shown positive results in reducing HF hospitalization and mortality

questions.verifiedExplanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) carry a Class 1A recommendation for HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. The DAPA-HF trial (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced trial (empagliflozin) both demonstrated reduced cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization in patients with and without diabetes. SGLT2 inhibitors are now considered one of the four pillars of HFrEF GDMT (alongside beta-blockers, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, and MRAs). They have a Class 2A recommendation for HFmrEF and HFpEF as well. Their mechanism in HF extends beyond glucose lowering — they promote natriuresis, reduce preload, improve cardiac energetics, and decrease myocardial fibrosis. Contraindications include symptomatic hypotension, SBP <95 mmHg, and eGFR <30.
verifiedReferences: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]
issuesFound: Answer text is truncated: 'Both diabetic AND non' — should read 'Both diabetic AND non-diabetic patients'. Fix in quiz bank.

questions.correctAnswer: Atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: In HFpEF, myocardial relaxation decreases, and the stiffness of the ventricle increases due to an increase in ventricular afterload.
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: statpearls-hf-ef: 'Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction is associated with hypertrophy and abnormal lusitropy'
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: S4 is the atrial gallop — occurs in late diastole when the atrium contracts against a stiff, noncompliant ventricle (diastolic dysfunction). S3 is the ventricular gallop — occurs in early diastole from rapid filling of a dilated ventricle (volume overload/systolic dysfunction). The StatPearls articles describe the underlying pathophysiology (ventricular stiffness in HFpEF) that produces the S4 sound.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: S4 = atrial kick against stiff ventricle is established cardiac auscultation physiology.

primary.supportingQuote: In HFpEF, myocardial relaxation decreases, and the stiffness of the ventricle increases

supporting.keyFinding: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction is associated with hypertrophy and abnormal lusitropy

questions.verifiedExplanation: The S4 gallop (atrial gallop) is a low-frequency sound occurring in late diastole, just before S1, produced by forceful atrial contraction against a stiff, noncompliant ventricle. It is heard best with the bell of the stethoscope at the apex. S4 is characteristic of diastolic dysfunction (HFpEF) — conditions like hypertensive heart disease, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and aortic stenosis that cause ventricular hypertrophy and decreased compliance. This contrasts with the S3 gallop (ventricular gallop), which occurs in early diastole from rapid filling of a volume-overloaded, dilated ventricle — characteristic of systolic dysfunction (HFrEF). S4 is absent in atrial fibrillation (no organized atrial contraction). MVP produces a mid-systolic click, not a gallop. VSD produces a holosystolic murmur.
verifiedReferences: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: BNP may be FALSELY LOW in which condition?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Adipose tissue also degrades natriuretic peptides.
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: statpearls-hf-ef from search results: 'NT-pro-brain natriuretic peptide exceeding 125 pg/ml and a brain natriuretic peptide equal to or greater than 35 pg/ml are reliable indicators for assessing heart failure.' (baseline thresholds imply that false lows are clinically significant)
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: CRITICAL teaching point: Adipose tissue expresses neprilysin, which degrades natriuretic peptides. This means obese patients with HF may have falsely low BNP levels, leading to missed diagnosis. The distractors actually INCREASE BNP: age (higher baselines), afib (atrial stretch), renal failure (decreased clearance).

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: Obesity causing falsely low BNP is well-established and acknowledged in current guidelines.

primary.supportingQuote: Adipose tissue also degrades natriuretic peptides

questions.verifiedExplanation: Obesity causes falsely low BNP levels because adipose tissue expresses neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades natriuretic peptides (BNP and ANP). This is clinically important: obese patients with genuine heart failure may have BNP levels below typical diagnostic thresholds, leading to missed or delayed diagnosis. Lower BNP cutoffs should be used in obese patients. The distractors all INCREASE BNP: advanced age raises baseline BNP (higher thresholds needed in elderly), atrial fibrillation elevates BNP through atrial stretch and neurohormonal activation, and renal failure elevates BNP due to decreased renal clearance (especially NT-proBNP, which is exclusively renally cleared). Understanding what raises vs. lowers BNP is essential for accurate clinical interpretation.
verifiedReferences: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: Acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. First-line treatment?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Diuretics recommended in any heart failure with symptoms and signs suggestive of volume overload. It relieves congestion, improve symptoms, and prevents worsening of heart failure. Loop diuretics are preferred over thiazide diuretics.
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: statpearls-hf: 'For patients with congestion, diuretics improve symptoms and reduce HF progression.'
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: IV loop diuretics (furosemide) are universally the first-line for acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. IV metoprolol would be harmful in acute decompensation (negative inotrope). IV NS would worsen volume overload. Cardioversion is only indicated for hemodynamically unstable arrhythmia, not routine ADHF.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: IV diuretics remain first-line for ADHF with congestion.

primary.supportingQuote: Diuretics recommended in any heart failure with symptoms and signs suggestive of volume overload. Loop diuretics are preferred over thiazide diuretics

questions.verifiedExplanation: IV furosemide (a loop diuretic) is the first-line treatment for acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. It acts within minutes when given IV, producing rapid diuresis and preload reduction. Initial IV dosing should be at least equivalent to the patient's home oral dose; diuretic-naive patients typically receive 20-40 mg IV. Additional therapies include IV nitroglycerin (afterload and preload reduction) and non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) for respiratory support. IV metoprolol would be harmful in acute decompensation — beta-blockers are negative inotropes that should not be initiated during active decompensation (they are continued only if the patient was previously on a stable dose). IV normal saline would worsen pulmonary edema. Immediate cardioversion is only indicated for hemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias.
verifiedReferences: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

questions.stem: In HFrEF, sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) is preferred over ACEi because:
questions.correctAnswer: PARADIGM-HF showed superior mortality and HF hospitalization reduction
distractors: It replaces the need for beta-blockers
It has fewer side effects than all other HF drugs
It is cheaper and more widely available

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI) sacubitril/valsartan demonstrated reduced cardiovascular and all-cause mortality in HFrEF compared to enalapril in the Prospective Comparison of ARNI with ACE-I to Determine Impact on Global Mortality and Morbidity in Heart Failure (PARADIGM-HF) trial.
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: statpearls-hf: 'Neprilysin is an enzyme that breaks down several hormones, including BNP, ANP, and bradykinin; it targets several novel therapeutics.'
layer1_sourceVerification.notes: PARADIGM-HF is explicitly named in StatPearls. The trial showed 20% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalization vs enalapril.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: ARNI is Class I recommendation for HFrEF per 2022 guidelines, preferred over ACEi/ARB.

primary.supportingQuote: sacubitril/valsartan demonstrated reduced cardiovascular and all-cause mortality in HFrEF compared to enalapril in the PARADIGM-HF trial

supporting.keyFinding: Neprilysin breaks down BNP, ANP, and bradykinin; neprilysin inhibition (sacubitril) must always be combined with ARB, not ACEi, due to angioedema risk

questions.verifiedExplanation: The PARADIGM-HF trial demonstrated that sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) reduced cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization by 20% compared to enalapril (ACEi) in HFrEF patients. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades beneficial natriuretic peptides (BNP, ANP) and bradykinin, while valsartan blocks the RAAS. This dual mechanism provides superior neurohormonal modulation. ARNI does NOT replace beta-blockers — all four pillars of GDMT (ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) should be used together. ARNI has a unique safety concern: it must never be combined with ACEi (36-hour washout required) due to severe angioedema risk. ARNI is more expensive than generic ACEi, not cheaper. The PARAGON-HF trial also showed benefit in HFpEF, particularly in women.
verifiedReferences: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

truncatedAnswers: Q376: 'Both diabetic AND non' should be 'Both diabetic AND non-diabetic patients'
auditSummary.trialReferencesNote: Trial names are widely cited in StatPearls and open-access guidelines. Used as supporting context per methodology exception for landmark trials.
